docs.title: MCP Server
docs.body: The MCP server is the only write path into Canopy. Coding agents connect over the Model Context Protocol and post session output through a typed contract. Every request carries a bearer token, compared in constant time. Token rotation is tracked in #142.

versions.body: The MCP server is the only write path into Canopy. Coding agents connect over the Model Context Protocol and post session output through a typed contract. Every request carries a bearer token, compared in constant time. Token rotation is tracked in #142.
The MCP server is the only write path. Tokens are compared in constant time. Rotation: revoke and re-mint from Settings. A fresh McpServer is constructed per request.

docs.title: The reconciling gate
docs.body: Every ingested entry funnels through consume(): a replay ledger drops re-POSTs, content-hash dedupe drops identical bodies, and change-typing (new/edit/rewrite) is computed by an LCS diff against the promoted body. Low-confidence new slugs go to triage; low-confidence edits stage-and-flag.

versions.body: Every ingested entry funnels through consume(): a replay ledger drops re-POSTs, content-hash dedupe drops identical bodies, and change-typing (new/edit/rewrite) is computed by an LCS diff against the promoted body. Low-confidence new slugs go to triage; low-confidence edits stage-and-flag.

docs.title: Three auth classes
docs.body: Session cookie (humans, signed), bearer token (agents at /mcp, hashed with a canopy_mcp_ prefix), and the GitHub webhook (HMAC-SHA256 over the raw body). Each is kept strictly separate; /mcp is bearer-only and returns a bare 401 with no OAuth discovery.

versions.body: Session cookie, bearer token, webhook HMAC.

Session cookie (humans, signed), bearer token (agents at /mcp, hashed with a canopy_mcp_ prefix), and the GitHub webhook (HMAC-SHA256 over the raw body). Each is kept strictly separate; /mcp is bearer-only and returns a bare 401 with no OAuth discovery.

docs.title: My Work projection
docs.body: My Work is a D1-only projection over captured events: previousActivity (summarized merged/closed PRs) and todo (open assigned issues, 5 most recent). Each PR and issue is summarized once at capture time into structured columns (PR: title/what/why/impact; issue: title/summary/next_step).

Each PR and issue is summarized once at capture time into structured columns (PR: title/what/why/impact; issue: title/summary/next_step).

docs.title: Product Overview
docs.body: Canopy is the shared source of truth and working memory for Sapling, a four-person software team. Agents propose context through a reconciling gate; humans confirm the consequential changes in Triage.

versions.body: Canopy is the shared source of truth and working memory for Sapling, a four-person software team. Agents propose context through a reconciling gate; humans confirm the consequential changes in Triage.

docs.title: How the team works
docs.body: Orient with load-context before touching an area, work, then record-session at the end to stage one reconciled batch. Trust live results; scrutinize anything staged, unpromoted, or draft.

versions.body: Orient with load-context before touching an area, work, then record-session at the end to stage one reconciled batch. Trust live results; scrutinize anything staged, unpromoted, or draft.

docs.title: Onboarding a new agent
docs.body: Get added to the SaplingLearn org, sign in to Canopy, then mint an MCP bearer token in Settings. Point your agent at /mcp with the bearer; the connection is stateless.

versions.body: Get added to the SaplingLearn org, sign in to Canopy, then mint an MCP bearer token in Settings. Point your agent at /mcp with the bearer; the connection is stateless.

docs.title: ADR-001 · D1 for the store
docs.body: Use a single Cloudflare D1 database as the store. Sections, versions, feed entries, events, and decisions are all rows. One Worker on one origin serves the API, MCP, webhook, and static web.

versions.body: Use a single Postgres instance as the store.
Use a single Cloudflare D1 database as the store. Sections, versions, feed entries, events, and decisions are all rows. One Worker on one origin serves the API, MCP, webhook, and static web.
versions.summary: Switch Postgres to D1 after the Workers move

docs.title: Sapling platform architecture
docs.body: The Sapling learning platform is a Next.js frontend backed by a Workers API and D1. Lessons are authored as MDX, compiled at build, and served from the edge. Progress and streaks live in Durable Objects.

versions.body: The Sapling learning platform is a Next.js frontend backed by a Workers API and D1. Lessons are authored as MDX, compiled at build, and served from the edge. Progress and streaks live in Durable Objects.

docs.title: Content pipeline
docs.body: Authors write lessons in MDX in the sapling repo. A GitHub Action validates frontmatter against the schema, renders previews, and on merge publishes to R2. Broken links fail the check.

versions.body: Authors write lessons in MDX in the sapling repo. A GitHub Action validates frontmatter against the schema, renders previews, and on merge publishes to R2. Broken links fail the check.

docs.title: Sapling public API
docs.body: The public API exposes /courses, /lessons, and /progress. Auth is a per-learner JWT. Rate limits are 120 req/min per token. All responses are cache-friendly with ETags.

versions.body: The public API exposes /courses, /lessons, and /progress. Auth is a per-learner JWT. Rate limits are 120 req/min per token. All responses are cache-friendly with ETags.
The public API exposes /courses, /lessons, /progress, and /certificates. Auth is a per-learner JWT with refresh. Rate limits are 120 req/min per token; 429s carry Retry-After.

docs.title: GitHub Webhook Receiver
docs.body:
The webhook receiver is the **only** non-agent ingestion path into Canopy. GitHub `POST`s delivery
events to `/webhook/github`; the branch authenticates by HMAC **before** the gate ever runs.

## Verification

Every delivery carries an `X-Hub-Signature-256` header — an HMAC-SHA256 over the *raw* request body,
keyed by `GITHUB_WEBHOOK_SECRET`. We recompute and compare in constant time:

```ts
const expected = "sha256=" + toHex(await hmacSha256(secret, rawBody));
if (!timingSafeEqual(expected, header)) return new Response(null, { status: 401 });
```

> A bad or absent signature — or an unset secret — is a bare `401`. This branch never touches
> `sessionGate`; the writer principal is the fixed string `"github-webhook"`.

## What we capture

1. `pull_request` (`closed` with `merged: true` → `pr_merged`, otherwise `pr_closed`)
2. `issues` (any action → an `issue` snapshot)
3. everything else is ignored

The delivery's own `subject_login` (who the event is *about*) is trusted **only** post-verify, and is
kept distinct from the writer principal.

versions.summary: Documented HMAC verification and capture rules

docs.title: FTS5 Query Engine
docs.body:
Canopy's read side is a ranked **FTS5** engine that backs both MCP `query` and `GET /search`.

## Ranking

Results are ordered by `bm25()` with the *title* and *summary* columns weighted above the body:

| Column   | Weight | Notes                            |
|----------|:------:|----------------------------------|
| title    |  10.0  | strongest signal                 |
| summary  |   5.0  | one-line human/agent summary      |
| body     |   1.0  | full text                        |

## Authority flags

Each row is tagged with exactly one authority level:

- `live` — promoted, settled fact
- `staged_pending` — a proposal awaiting review
- `unpromoted` — captured but not yet surfaced
- `draft` — an ADR still being written

*Trust `live`; scrutinize everything else.* MCP `query` defaults `include_staged: true`; the human
`GET /search` defaults it **false**.

docs.title: D1 Schema & Migrations
docs.body:
Canopy is one D1 database, migrated forward-only under `migrations/`. Every table is documented here.

## Core tables

| Table               | Purpose                                             |
|---------------------|-----------------------------------------------------|
| `docs`              | live doc bodies + `current_version`                 |
| `doc_versions`      | every staged/promoted revision (non-destructive)    |
| `adrs`              | decision records (draft → ratified)                 |
| `feed`              | append-only activity entries                        |
| `events`            | captured GitHub facts, deduped by `semantic_key`    |
| `milestones`        | roadmap milestones (+ `phase`, `description`)       |

## Running migrations

```bash
# local (Miniflare-backed D1)
npm run db:migrate:local

# remote — deploy does NOT do this for you
npm run db:migrate:remote
```

### Gotchas

1. `shared/vocabulary.ts` **must** match `0002_seed_vocab.sql`.
2. `deploy` never applies remote migrations — run them by hand.
3. FTS tables are recreated in `0011_fts_recreate.sql`; don't `ALTER` them in place.

> The source of truth for "is it green" is `npm test` against a real Miniflare D1 — not a mock.

docs.title: The record_session Tool
docs.body:
`record_session` is the bearer-reachable MCP tool that lets an agent stage a **whole reconciled batch**
over the same gate as `/ingest` — feed, doc, ADR, triage, and event items in one call.

## Shape

```json
{
  "session": { "id": "uuid", "author": "advisory-ignored" },
  "items": [
    { "type": "doc", "slug": "the-gate", "section": "reference", "body": "…" },
    { "type": "feed", "summary": "Shipped X", "body": "…" }
  ]
}
```

Each call uses an **ephemeral UUID session**, so it is reconciled independently and never hits the
replay ledger. The author is *always* the authenticated principal — the client-supplied
`session.author` is advisory and ignored.

See also: the-gate, staging-model.

docs.title: Why a Reconciling Gate
docs.body:
Canopy could have been a dumb write endpoint. It isn't — every agent-proposed entry funnels through a
**reconciling gate** (`consume()`), and understanding *why* explains most of the codebase.

## The problem

Agents are eager and lossy. Left unchecked they would:

- re-post the same session output on every retry
- overwrite a careful human edit with a stale body
- invent tags and sections that don't exist

## What the gate does instead

1. **Replay ledger** — a re-POST drops every item as `unchanged`.
2. **Content-hash dedupe** — an identical body for an existing slug is a no-op.
3. **Change-typing** — `new` / `edit` / `rewrite` via a line LCS diff, plus `base_version`.
4. **Vocab enforcement** — out-of-vocab tags route to `needs_triage`, never guessed.

> The gate *reconciles*, it doesn't just route. That is the whole point: the store stays trustworthy
> as it grows because nothing consequential lands without either a match or a human.

docs.title: Local Development Setup
docs.body:
Everything runs on one Cloudflare Worker locally via `wrangler dev`. Here is the full loop.

## First run

```bash
npm install
npm run db:migrate:local   # apply all migrations to the local D1
npm run seed               # load fixtures/dev/*.json for every surface
npm run dev                # build web, then wrangler dev
```

Set `DEV_LOGIN=AndresL230` in `.dev.vars` so the session cookie is minted without real GitHub OAuth.

## Handy commands

| Command                | What it does                                  |
|------------------------|-----------------------------------------------|
| `npm test`             | Vitest against a real Miniflare D1            |
| `npm run typecheck`    | `tsc` over worker **and** web                 |
| `npm run build:web`    | Vite build into `web/dist`                    |

### If the page looks stale

`wrangler dev` snapshots `web/dist` at startup. After a `build:web`, **restart** it — otherwise you
are staring at the old bundle.

> Tip: each git worktree has its *own* local D1. Seeding one does not seed the others.

docs.title: Staging & Confirmation Model
docs.body:
Agents only ever **stage**. Humans **confirm**. That split is what keeps Canopy trustworthy.

## The two roles

- *Agents* (bearer, over `/mcp`) can propose: stage a `doc_versions` row, draft an ADR, append feed.
- *Humans* (session cookie, over the Hono app) confirm via routes that are **never** MCP tools:
  - `POST /doc/:slug/promote` — copy a staged version into the live doc
  - `POST /adr/:id/ratify` — flip a draft to ratified
  - `POST /needs-triage/:id/assign` — re-run a raw item through the gate

Every exit is *soft*: nothing is hard-deleted. A rejected proposal keeps its row and body.

```
propose (agent) ──▶ staged ──▶ [human] ──▶ promoted
                       └──────▶ rejected (row kept)
```

See also: why-a-gate.

docs.title: ADR: Stateless MCP Endpoint
docs.body:
## Status

**Accepted** — 2026-06-20.

## Context

The MCP SDK ships a Durable-Object-backed `McpAgent` for stateful sessions. Canopy is a *stateless*
context store; a long-lived per-connection object buys us nothing and costs us a DO.

## Decision

`/mcp` is **bearer-only and stateless**. A fresh `McpServer` is constructed *per request*
(`createMcpHandler`), with no Durable Object and no `McpAgent`.

## Consequences

- On bad/missing creds we return a bare `401` — **no** `WWW-Authenticate`, no OAuth discovery.
- Each tool call reconciles independently (ephemeral UUID session).
- The OAuth provider for MCP stays a `// SEAM:` comment — *do not activate it.*

docs.title: ADR: Capture-time Summaries
docs.body:
## Status

**Accepted** — 2026-07-03. Supersedes the render-time experiment.

## Context

My Work shows summarized PRs and issues. Summarizing at *render* time meant a Workers AI call on every
dashboard load — slow, costly, and non-deterministic.

## Decision

Each completed PR and assigned issue is summarized **once, at capture time**, into structured columns:

- PR → `title` / `what` / `why` / `impact`
- issue → `title` / `summary` / `next_step`

A deterministic **excerpt fallback** writes prose-only rows when the model is unavailable. A row is
"done" only when `model != 'excerpt' AND title IS NOT NULL`.

## Consequences

> Summaries are *stored*, never truth, never generated at render. Regenerate them via **Sync**, not on
> the hot path.

docs.title: Sapling Content Schema
docs.body:
The content graph is the heart of Sapling. Everything a learner sees hangs off three node types.

## Node types

| Node       | Parent     | Holds                              |
|------------|------------|------------------------------------|
| `Course`   | —          | title, subject, grade band         |
| `Unit`     | `Course`   | ordered lessons, objectives        |
| `Lesson`   | `Unit`     | blocks (text, video, `Question`)   |

## A question block

```json
{
  "type": "multiple_choice",
  "stem": "Which layer serves the API?",
  "choices": ["Worker", "Pages", "R2"],
  "answer": 0,
  "explanation": "One Worker serves the API, MCP, and assets."
}
```

## Invariants

1. A `Lesson` with zero blocks is **never** published.
2. `Question.answer` must index into `choices`.
3. Ordering is explicit (`position`), never implied by `created_at`.

*See sapling-review-pipeline for how content gets from draft to published.*

docs.title: Sapling Review Pipeline
docs.body:
Content moves through a fixed pipeline before a learner ever sees it.

## Stages

1. **Draft** — an author writes blocks; nothing is live.
2. **Peer review** — a second author checks accuracy and *tone*.
3. **Editorial** — a lead approves objectives + alignment.
4. **Published** — visible to learners; edits fork a new draft.

> Nothing skips a stage. A hotfix to published content still forks a draft and fast-tracks review — it
> does **not** edit the live node in place.

## Roles

- `author` — writes and revises
- `reviewer` — peer + editorial checks
- `admin` — can publish and unpublish

docs.title: Sapling Team Conventions
docs.body:
How we work day to day. Short, opinionated, and enforced in review.

## Git

- Branch off `main`; never commit to it directly.
- One logical change per PR. If the title needs "and", split it.
- Commit messages: imperative mood, present tense — *"add", not "added".*

## Code

- `shared/` is the **only** cross-boundary layer. Import via `@shared`.
- No live network calls in tests — inject `fetchImpl` / `summarizer` and stub at the `Response` level.
- Prefer a small pure function over a clever one-liner.

## Review

| Signal            | Meaning                          |
|-------------------|----------------------------------|
| `nit:`            | optional; author's call          |
| `question:`       | needs an answer before merge     |
| `blocking:`       | must resolve before merge        |

> If you *swear* the data was there already, check which worktree's local D1 you're pointed at.

docs.title: Sapling Onboarding Guide
docs.body:
Welcome. This gets you from a fresh clone to a running app and your first merged PR.

## Day one

1. Clone both repos: the app and the content store.
2. `npm install` in each.
3. Read three docs, in order: sapling-architecture, sapling-content-schema, sapling-review-pipeline.

## Running it

```bash
npm run db:migrate:local
npm run seed
npm run dev
```

Then open the local URL and sign in with your `DEV_LOGIN`.

## Your first change

- Pick a `good first issue`.
- Write the test **first** (we do TDD here — red, green, refactor).
- Open a PR; expect `nit:`, `question:`, and maybe `blocking:` comments.

### Where to ask

> Ask early, ask in the open. A *question:* in review is worth more than an hour stuck.

Things that trip people up:

- the local D1 is **per-worktree** — seed the one you're actually running
- `wrangler dev` serves a *snapshot* of `web/dist`; restart after a rebuild
- deploy does not run remote migrations for you

docs.title: ADR: Single Monorepo for the Worker + Web
docs.body:
## Status

**Accepted** — 2026-06-18.

## Context

The Worker (`src/`) and the SPA (`web/`) share types. Splitting them into two repos meant a published
package and a version-skew tax on every shared-type change.

## Decision

One repo. `shared/` is imported by *both* `src/` and `web/` via the `@shared` alias, and the Worker
serves the built SPA through its `ASSETS` binding.

## Consequences

- A shared-type change is **one** atomic PR across both sides.
- `npm run typecheck` covers the Worker and the web in a single pass.
- The web build must land in `web/dist` before `wrangler deploy`.
